author: @larrypenoux9065
text:
The maths for this will be dizzying at first and then may become 
quite embarrassing for some people- particularly Hertz and Marconi I think.

TV Aerials are supposed to be fairly large. This one you've made is minuscule by comparison! The surface area of the aluminium hardly compares to that of a 
standard TV antenna. You appear to be picking up a very solid signal at something 
like a 256th wave resonance. Not a half wave nor a quarter wave antenna as 
most of them are made. Ground breaking science here.

Look at the smallness of the thing, people! Holy Mother of God. I have never 
seen the like.

Fascinating is an understatement, mate. You've really got something.
That you've just "put it out there" as prior art is so humbling to watch. 
I want to be more like you.

Truly heroic!

Bravo!

text: Wow! OMG! That's the elevated antenna plate Tesla described. Are you connecting to the inner GO layer and is it totally isolated from atmosphere? Or are you connected to the two aluminum plates? Please do a video with a larger sheet. The ink might also work with that arrangement. Awesome Robert.

replies.author: @TerryHope
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
.."Here's the interesting part. If we wish to receive power rather than signals, a critical issue arises.
Driving a tiny antenna with a large signal will create large currents and heat the antenna. Small antennas are inefficient when compared to half-wave dipoles. If we wish to maximize the virtual aperature of a really tiny antenna (e.g. make our 10KHz pie-plate coil act 10KM across,) we'll quickly be frustrated by wire heating. All the extra received energy will go into warming the copper. Possible solutions: use superconductor loops, or at low frequencies use the nearest equivalent to an AC-driven superconductor: a rotating permanent magnet or rotating capacitor plates."... http://amasci.com/tesla/tesceive.html

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Terry Hope
i thought that was a result of impedance mismatching?

replies.author: @TerryHope
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
I like this quote "Suppose that a storm (or even the entire Earth) has a very strong vertical AC electrostatic field. The loop antennas on VLF radios would not detect it. Horizontal dipoles would not detect it. However, a resonant circuit connected to a suspended wire (and to ground) certainly would. With a high-Q resonant circuit, the antenna might even receive significant power. Call it the "artificial ball-lightning" analogy"...

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Terry Hope
i like it too mate - cheers for that

author: @cheerdiver
text:
Make an array for Db gain.  Looked into optimizing spec freq for distance, on the "coat hanger" design.  Basic things like "whisker" length could be changed, including reflector distance.
Find a broadcaster in the 100 Km range, find it's broadcast freq.  Even a basic choke can be made by wrapping the connecting wire around a cardboard tube.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: none of that makes sense to me - what on earth is an array for Db gain? - remember i am not an antenna guy and you need to dumb it down for me

replies.author: @cheerdiver
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Arranging the receivers in parallel will increase the signal gain.  The physical size, of each plate, will determine optimum freq for which it will receive.  It will still receive most freqs, but if you have a specific station that is far off tune the antenna for that station.  An alt method of "tuning" is to change the leads capacitance, wrapping the leads around in a circle will accomplish said change.  I am not a freq tech, just some basics picked up in trying to make my own antenna for a long distance station.  Didn't get accomplish the goal, but learned some basics in the attempt.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
cheerdiver
cheers mate - i understand now lol
